title: RAG as AI Memory Architecture — Quick Check

questions.q: What is the difference between parametric memory and contextual memory in an LLM, and which type does RAG augment?
questions.a: Parametric memory is knowledge baked into the model's weights during training — facts the model 'knows' without any retrieval. Contextual memory is knowledge provided in the prompt context window at inference time. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) augments contextual memory by retrieving relevant documents and inserting them into the prompt context. RAG does not modify the model's weights; it expands what the model has access to in the current request.

questions.q: Walk through the write path of Meridian's memory system: from a user action to a stored embedding.
questions.a: 1. User interaction occurs (e.g. writes a document, completes a task, gives AI feedback). 2. A memory-worthy event is detected (by pattern matching or AI scoring). 3. The relevant text is extracted and sent to an embedding model (e.g. `text-embedding-3-small`) which returns a high-dimensional vector. 4. The text + embedding + metadata (user_id, workspace_id, source_type, created_at) are inserted into the `memory_fragments` table. 5. The pgvector index updates automatically for future similarity searches.

questions.q: What is the context window budget problem in RAG, and how does Meridian's context assembler address it?
questions.a: Each LLM request has a fixed context window (e.g. 200k tokens for Claude). A naive RAG implementation might retrieve 20 memory chunks + the full conversation history + instructions, potentially exceeding the limit or sending low-value content. Meridian's context assembler applies a budget: it ranks retrieved chunks by similarity score, truncates conversation history to the most recent N turns, and assembles only as much context as fits within the reserved token budget, always preserving the system prompt and user message.

questions.q: Why does Meridian's RAG search filter by BOTH `user_id` and `workspace_id`? What security property does each filter enforce?
questions.a: `workspace_id` enforces multi-tenancy: prevents User A in Workspace 1 from retrieving memories stored by anyone in Workspace 2. `user_id` enforces per-user privacy within a workspace: prevents User A in Workspace 1 from retrieving User B's personal memories even though they share the workspace. Both filters are required simultaneously. Using only workspace_id would allow cross-user memory leakage within a workspace; using only user_id would allow cross-workspace leakage if a user is a member of multiple workspaces.
